title: Canarium oleosum
taxonomy: Canarium oleosum is classified as a species of plant in the family Burseraceae, a family of flowering plants commonly known as the torchwood or frankincense trees. It belongs to the genus Canarium, which comprises about 50 species of plants native to tropical and subtropical regions of the world. The plant is native to the Pacific Islands, particularly in Fiji and Samoa. Canarium oleosum is a small to medium-sized tree or shrub that grows in a variety of habitats, from coastal to inland areas. The plant's classification is further supported by its distinct morphological characteristics and molecular features.
uses: Canarium oleosum is traditionally used in various parts of the world for its medicinal properties, including treating fever, cough, and respiratory issues. The plant is also valued for its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which are believed to aid in the healing process. In some cultures, Canarium oleosum is used to treat skin conditions and wounds. The plant's essential oil is also used to relieve pain and reduce inflammation. Further research is needed to confirm the effectiveness of Canarium oleosum in modern medicine.

intro: Canarium oleosum, also known as the Malabar tamarind or Indian tamarind, is a plant species in the family Sapindaceae that is native to tropical Asia.
